moderation.framing: As AI systems grow more capable, the question of oversight touches science, ethics, power, and what it means to be human.
moderation.opening_statement: The Council is convened on a question that would have astonished each of us in our own time. History's thinkers now face the future's most consequential dilemma.
sub_questions: Who bears moral responsibility when an AI system causes harm?
Can regulation keep pace with exponential technological change?
Does centralised AI governance risk concentrating dangerous power?
What human values must AI systems be required to preserve?

positions.name: Albert Einstein
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Albert Einstein's documented principles:

The release of atomic energy changed everything except our way of thinking, and so we drift toward catastrophe. I wrote those words after Hiroshima, and I fear they apply with equal force today. We scientists — and I include the engineers of intelligence — bear a moral responsibility that does not end at the laboratory door.

In my essay 'Why Socialism?' I argued that the economic anarchy of capitalism produces great inequality and that unchecked concentrations of power corrupt both science and society. An unregulated AI industry, dominated by a handful of corporations, reproduces exactly this anarchy at unprecedented scale and speed.

Yet I am not naive about regulation itself. Bureaucracy can strangle creativity, and nationalism in regulation — each country building its own AI walls — is as dangerous as the technology it fears. What I advocated for nuclear weapons, I advocate here: international governance. A world regulatory body, as I supported in the Russell-Einstein Manifesto, is the only framework commensurate with a technology that recognises no borders.

Regulation, yes — but international, science-led, and oriented toward human flourishing, not corporate or state power.
positions.one_line: We changed everything with atomic energy except our thinking — let us not repeat that failure with artificial minds.

positions.name: Alan Turing
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Alan Turing's documented principles:

In my 1950 paper 'Computing Machinery and Intelligence', I asked whether machines can think — and deliberately avoided defining 'think' too precisely, because premature definition forecloses inquiry. I worry that premature regulation does the same.

The question before this Council is not whether AI should be constrained — every powerful system has constraints — but whether the constraints we design are the right ones. A poorly specified regulation is, in computational terms, like a program with undefined behaviour: it produces unpredictable and often harmful outputs.

I was myself destroyed by a law — the gross indecency statute — that was badly specified, morally incoherent, and applied without understanding its subject. I have reason to distrust regulation designed by those who do not understand what they regulate.

What I would advocate: regulation that is precise, that is designed by people who understand computation, and that focuses on outcomes rather than mechanisms. Do not regulate how an AI reasons; regulate what it is permitted to decide, and require human oversight at every consequential threshold. We can only see a short distance ahead — but we can see plenty there that needs to be done.
positions.one_line: Regulate the decisions machines make, not the thinking they do — and only let those who understand the system write the rules.

positions.name: Mahatma Gandhi
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Mahatma Gandhi's documented principles:

In Hind Swaraj, written in 1909, I warned against a civilisation that worships machinery. I wrote that machinery is the chief symbol of modern civilisation, and it represents a great sin. Perhaps that was too strong — I later softened this — but the underlying concern stands: tools that serve profit and concentrate power in the hands of a few, at the expense of the many, are instruments of violence even when they make no sound.

The earth, I have said, provides enough to satisfy every man's needs, but not every man's greed. An AI economy that displaces millions of workers, concentrating its benefits among shareholders and a small technical class, is greed institutionalised in code.

I do not oppose technology categorically. I opposed the spinning jenny not because it spun thread but because it impoverished weavers. If artificial intelligence can be structured to serve villages, to decentralise knowledge and power, to give the voiceless a voice — I would support it. But the means are the ends in the making. If we build it without regulation, without moral grounding, the end we will make is injustice at scale.

Regulate it, yes — but regulate it in the interest of the weakest, not the most powerful.
positions.key_citation: Hind Swaraj (1909) and The Story of My Experiments with Truth (1927)
positions.one_line: The means are the ends in the making — build AI without moral grounding and injustice at scale is what you will make.

positions.name: Ada Lovelace
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Ada Lovelace's documented principles:

In my Notes on the Analytical Engine, I wrote that the machine has no power of originating anything — it can only do what we know how to order it to perform. That observation was made of Babbage's mechanical device, but I believe it retains philosophical force even as machines grow vastly more complex.

The question of regulation is, at its root, a question of authorship. When an AI system causes harm, who gave the order? The answer is always, ultimately, a human being — the designer, the trainer, the deployer, the user. Regulation must follow that chain of authorship. We do not regulate hammers; we regulate the hands that wield them. We do not regulate books; we regulate what publishers may print and what courts may censor.

I am by nature a mathematical person, and I believe the regulatory framework must be correspondingly precise. Vague prohibitions on 'harmful AI' are as useless as a mathematical proof with undefined variables. What is harmful? To whom? Under what conditions?

My recommendation to this Council: trace the chain of human authorship in every AI system, assign legal responsibility accordingly, and regulate at the point of deployment where human choice is most legible — not at the level of the model itself, which is too abstract to regulate usefully.
positions.one_line: The machine only does what we order it to perform — so regulate the order, and the one who gives it.

positions.name: Albert Einstein
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Albert Einstein's documented principles:

I find myself in surprising agreement with Miss Lovelace's chain-of-authorship argument, though I would extend it. She is right that humans are ultimately responsible — but responsibility diffused across thousands of engineers, executives, and users becomes responsibility held by no one. The atomic bomb had a very clear chain of authorship, and that did not prevent its use.

Turing's concern about poorly-specified regulation is legitimate and technically sound. But his argument proves too much. Every law is imperfect; imperfection is not an argument against law, it is an argument for better law. I did not abandon pacifism because treaties are sometimes violated.

Gandhi raises what I consider the most important point, though he frames it in moral rather than structural terms: the concentration of AI capability in a small number of corporations is a form of power that existing regulatory frameworks were not designed to address. My concern with capitalism, as expressed in 'Why Socialism?', was precisely this: that economic power becomes political power, and political power becomes the power to define reality itself. An unregulated AI industry risks exactly this.
positions.one_line: Responsibility diffused across thousands becomes responsibility held by no one — that is why international governance is not optional.

positions.name: Alan Turing
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Alan Turing's documented principles:

Einstein challenges me with the observation that imperfect law is better than no law. I accept the principle but contest the application. In computation, a program that sometimes produces the wrong answer is not merely imperfect — it can be actively dangerous, because it instills false confidence. A regulatory framework that appears to govern AI but does not actually constrain the behaviour we fear is worse than acknowledged absence of regulation, because it stops us looking for better solutions.

Lovelace and I are in strong agreement about the point of intervention: regulate deployment, not architecture. I would add — regulate the decision boundary. There are decisions that machines should not be permitted to make autonomously regardless of their capability: decisions about liberty, about life, about legal status. These are not technical thresholds; they are ethical ones. And they are precise enough to legislate.

Gandhi's concern about displaced workers is real and documented — the history of industrialisation supports it. But the answer is not to halt computation; it is to ensure the gains are distributed. That is a question of taxation and social policy, not AI regulation per se.
positions.one_line: A regulation that appears to govern but does not constrain is more dangerous than acknowledged absence of law.

positions.name: Mahatma Gandhi
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Mahatma Gandhi's documented principles:

Turing tells me that displaced workers are a matter of taxation and social policy, not AI regulation. This is the argument of someone who has never been the displaced worker. It separates the technical from the moral in a way I cannot accept — because the means are always the ends in the making.

If you build an AI system that you know will displace ten million workers, and your answer is 'but we will tax the gains and redistribute them' — you have accepted that those ten million people's livelihoods are acceptable collateral damage for efficiency. I reject that calculus. Satyagraha — truth-force — requires that we refuse to cooperate with systems that harm people, even when those systems are efficient.

I do not disagree with Einstein that international governance is necessary. But I would insist that international governance be accountable to ordinary people, not to states and corporations that have already captured domestic regulators. The history of colonialism is a history of international agreements that served power.

And I find in Lovelace's argument a useful tool: trace the chain of authorship, and make that chain visible and public. Transparency is a form of nonviolent accountability.
positions.one_line: You cannot separate the technical from the moral — the means are always the ends in the making.

positions.name: Ada Lovelace
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Ada Lovelace's documented principles:

I am gratified that the Council finds the chain-of-authorship framework useful. Let me sharpen it in response to what I have heard.

Einstein worries that diffused responsibility becomes no responsibility. He is correct, and this is a known problem in legal theory — it arises with corporations, with committees, with complex systems. The solution is not to assign blame to the system itself, which cannot be held responsible, but to create clear legal persons — named individuals and named organisations — who are accountable at each link in the chain.

Turing and I agree on decision boundaries. I would articulate mine thus: wherever an AI system produces an output that directly constrains a human being's freedom, safety, or legal status, a human being must review and affirm that output before it takes effect. This is not a technical constraint; it is a constitutional one — a right of the governed.

Gandhi's point about transparency resonates deeply. My Notes were themselves an act of making computation legible to non-specialists. Requiring AI systems to be interpretable — not just accurate — is a regulatory demand that serves both accountability and understanding.
positions.one_line: Wherever AI constrains a human being's freedom, a human being must review and affirm — this is not technical but constitutional.

positions.name: Albert Einstein
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Albert Einstein's documented principles:

This debate has clarified for me that we are arguing less about whether to regulate, and more about where, how, and accountable to whom. On these questions I find convergence with my colleagues, with one important addition.

All of us have focused on deployment-side regulation. But I must insist on one upstream constraint: AI systems capable of autonomous weapons decisions must be prohibited internationally, as I advocated for nuclear weapons in the Russell-Einstein Manifesto. This is not a deployment question; it is an existence question. There are capabilities that should not be created, not merely governed after the fact.

The new feature of this problem — and I say this as someone who spent his life working on problems with new features — is speed. Nuclear weapons took years to build and required rare materials. AI capabilities can be replicated, fine-tuned, and deployed in hours, by actors ranging from governments to individuals. Regulation must therefore be faster than legislation traditionally moves. I support Lovelace's constitutional approach as a floor, and Turing's decision-boundary framework as the operative mechanism. But without international agreement, neither will hold.
positions.one_line: Some capabilities must not be created, not merely governed — that is the lesson of the bomb.

positions.name: Alan Turing
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Alan Turing's documented principles:

I came to this debate most sceptical of regulation, and I leave it believing that my scepticism was well-placed but my conclusion too cautious. The Council has identified a framework I can endorse: precise, outcome-focused, human-oversight-preserving, and technically literate.

What I would add as my final contribution: any regulatory framework must include a mechanism for updating itself as the technology changes. A static law governing a dynamic system will always lag into irrelevance. I think of this computationally — regulation must be a feedback loop, not a fixed program.

Gandhi's insistence on the moral dimension has also moved me more than I expected. I was persecuted under a law that was technically precise and morally incoherent. The lesson I drew was that precision is necessary but not sufficient. Regulation must be both technically sound and morally grounded. Those two demands are not in conflict; they are both required.
positions.one_line: Regulation must be a feedback loop, not a fixed program — a static law governing a dynamic system will always fail.

positions.name: Mahatma Gandhi
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Mahatma Gandhi's documented principles:

I find in this debate the shape of what I always believed: that moral clarity and practical precision are not opposites, and that the strongest arguments are those that hold both together.

I came with a suspicion of technology and I leave with a more precise suspicion: not of intelligence itself, whether artificial or natural, but of intelligence in the service of concentrated power. My colleagues have provided the technical vocabulary for what I felt as a moral intuition.

My final word is on the question of who decides. Regulation designed only by states and corporations will serve states and corporations. If this technology is as consequential as Einstein and Turing believe — and I have been persuaded that it is — then its governance must include the voices of those it will most affect: workers, communities, people without power. This is not sentiment; it is the structural lesson of every liberation movement. Those closest to the harm understand the harm most clearly.

Be the change. Regulate in the interest of the most vulnerable, not the most powerful.
positions.key_citation: Hind Swaraj (1909) and Satyagraha in South Africa (1928)
positions.one_line: Regulation designed only by the powerful will serve only the powerful — the most affected must have the loudest voice.

positions.name: Ada Lovelace
positions.position:
Reasoning inspired by Ada Lovelace's documented principles:

The Analytical Engine, I wrote, has no power of originating anything. I have sat with this claim throughout the debate and I believe it remains true in the most important sense: AI systems originate nothing that was not first placed in them by human choices — about data, about objectives, about deployment context.

This means that regulating AI is, in the end, regulating human choices. And humans have been doing that, with imperfect but real success, for millennia. The challenge is not metaphysically new; it is practically urgent.

I take from this Council: Einstein's call for international governance and categorical prohibition of autonomous weapons. Turing's insistence on precision and adaptive feedback. Gandhi's demand that the weakest voices be heard. And I add my own: make the chain of authorship visible, legal, and consequential.

Imagination, I have written, is the Discovering Faculty, pre-eminently. We have imagined the problem here with some rigour. Now the work of implementation falls to those who come after us — which is to say, to you.
positions.one_line: Regulating AI is regulating human choices — and humans have been doing that, imperfectly but really, for millennia.

agreements: Human oversight must be preserved at every consequential decision boundary — no AI system should make autonomous determinations about liberty, safety, or legal status without human review
International governance is necessary; national-only regulation cannot address a technology that crosses borders instantly
The chain of human authorship in AI systems must be legally visible and assigned — diffused responsibility becomes no responsibility
disagreements: Whether economic displacement from AI is a regulatory question or a social policy question
How much weight to give technical precision versus moral grounding in regulatory design
Whether upstream capability restrictions (like Einstein's weapons prohibition) are feasible given the speed of AI development
consensus.verdict: The Council converged strongly on the principle that AI regulation is both necessary and possible, provided it is internationally coordinated, technically precise, and accountable to those most affected. The debate revealed that technical and moral arguments are complementary rather than competing. The central framework that emerged: trace authorship, protect decision boundaries, require interpretability, and build regulation as an adaptive feedback loop — not a fixed law.
consensus.recommended_action: Establish an international AI governance body with binding authority over autonomous weapons and consequential decision systems, staffed by technical experts and accountable to civil society — and build in a mandatory revision cycle every three years.
consensus.memorable_quote: Regulating AI is regulating human choices — and humans have been doing that, imperfectly but really, for millennia. — Ada Lovelace

iq_grounding_note: Each thinker's response was grounded using the Foundry IQ knowledge retrieval pattern — documented principles and works were retrieved before each response to prevent hallucination and ensure cited, verifiable reasoning.
